references.title: Enhancing the Accuracy of Predicting Students Grades in Open-Ended Questions through Adjustments to Attention Weights

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The authors made 5 questions to the students every week and used TF-IDF to create textual representation of the answers of students.

This is followed by a Transformer Encoder model to assign different weights to each word.
extracted.Comments:
Learners had to answer these 5 questions every week of the semester.

Q1: Please explain today’s content in your own words.  Q2: Write down what you understood and what you were able to do from today’s content.  Q3: Write down what you did not understand or were not able to do from today’s content.  Q4: If you have any questions, please write them down.  Q5: Write down your thoughts or reflections on today’s lesson.

Authors make a comparison between the use the raw answers vs the approach → model performance is significantly better.

Authors also show the top 20 most important variables based on being the ones with the higher weight.
extracted.Limitations:
- Subject to bias based on learner’s differences in vocabulary, 

- Small sample size

- Holdout method implementation

annotations.text: Increased digitalization of learning environments has allowed for the use of Log data to predict performance. However, these models requiure a larger-scale system that continuously collects and updates the model with new data. In order to have an easier system for data collection, the authors extract lecture questions from a Japanese University and use them to classify student grades.

The authors also attempt to improve the performance of the model by utilizing a TF-IDF representation of the text answers. The goal here is to identify words that are indicative to the student’s grade. The resultts show that proposed methodoly represents a slight improvement over the baseline models tested.
